Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

medical.title: ATS: City-Wide mold intervention can reduce ED asthma visits
medical.summary: A city-wide mold intervention in public housing can reduce asthma emergency department (ED) visits, according to a study presented at the American Thoracic Society 2026 International Conference, held from May 15 to 20 in Orlando, Florida.

medical.title: Prehospital trauma care gets flexible option as whole blood matches components in nationwide trial
medical.summary: Giving whole blood or the component parts of blood are equally effective options for paramedics and emergency medical technicians to use in treating patients with severe, traumatic bleeding before arriving at the hospital, according to a large, nationwide trial directed by University of Pittsburgh and UPMC clinicians and scientists.

medical.title: New-onset A-Fib may accelerate kidney function decline
medical.summary: New-onset atrial fibrillation (AF) may accelerate kidney function decline, according to a study published online May 14 in JAMA Network Open.

medical.title: More than a third of menopausal women lose sleep to hot flashes, waking during the night
medical.summary: While sleep is essential to health, women face unique challenges to getting quality sleep across key life stages. According to new survey results from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine, more than a third of women ages 45–64 report frequent nighttime waking (37%) and night sweats or hot flashes that disrupt sleep (35%) due to menopause, while more than a quarter have increased daytime fatigue (29%) and trouble falling asleep (27%). Only 6% of women in that age group say their sleep quality has not changed during menopause.

medical.title: From PCOS to PMOS: What the name change to polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome means for women's health
medical.summary: "Hopeful," "excited" and "helpful" for the future of women's health—these are just some of the words expressed by the team that worked together for more than a decade to change the name polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) to polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS).

medical.title: Why brain cells learn better: NMDA receptor maps may explain memory-linked calcium flow
medical.summary: The human brain constantly adapts in response to experiences, forming new connections between neurons and reorganizing existing ones. The brain's ability to adapt in response to experiences is known as neuroplasticity.

medical.title: Key multiple sclerosis models reveal different damage timelines, offering roadmap for myelin repair studies
medical.summary: More than 1 million people across the United States live with multiple sclerosis (MS), a disease that affects the brain, optic nerves, and spine. MS is an unpredictable disorder, with symptoms—such as overwhelming fatigue, muscle spasms, and vision problems—flaring up and then subsiding over days, months, or even years. To identify new treatment paradigms for MS, studying the underlying damage to the nervous system is key.

medical.title: Flavored vapes led to a major shake‑up at the FDA
medical.summary: The resignation of Marty Makary, commissioner of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, on May 12, 2026, brought to the forefront a heated controversy over fruit-flavored nicotine vapes.

science.title: Findings reconsider the existence of Europa's vapor plumes
science.summary: Looking back at 14 years of Hubble telescope data for Jupiter's moon Europa has given Southwest Research Institute (SwRI) scientists a better understanding of its tenuous atmosphere. The findings have cast doubt on previous evidence suggesting that the icy moon intermittently discharges faint water plumes from a presumed subsurface ocean.

science.title: Professional chess analysis reveals faster decisions correlate with higher quality moves
science.summary: In chess, faster decisions are on average of higher quality. This is the conclusion of a study that has just been published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. The team of researchers, which, in addition to Professor Uwe Sunde from LMU, includes scientists from Erasmus University Rotterdam and UniDistance Suisse, analyzed data from professional games of chess. Their aim was to investigate how the time taken to make a complex strategic decision is related to the quality of this decision. Sunde and his colleagues believe the outcome of their research indicates that the decision time reflects the subjectively perceived difficulty of the problem, which can vary depending on the situation.

science.title: Is Earth's constant companion a stray asteroid or a chunk of the moon?
science.summary: Earth has a group of cosmic stalkers. Known as "co-orbitals," these small bits of rock have a 1:1 mean motion resonance with Earth. Basically, they take the exact same amount of time to orbit the sun as we do. Astronomers have long believed these objects wandered in from the main asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, but recent spectral analysis suggests they better match the space-weathered lunar silicates that make up the moon's surface. As such, there has been an ongoing debate about whether these cosmic stalkers are actually visitors from the belt or blasted pieces of the moon. A new study, published in Icarus, from researchers Elisa Alessi and Robert Jedicke provides strong hints that the belt is the more likely source—but pretty soon we'll get a definitive answer from a spacecraft.

science.title: Hubble reveals rare galaxy 100 million light-years away caught in transition
science.summary: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image reveals an enigmatic galaxy with a bright center and a face that hints at spiral structure, yet it holds no obvious spiral arms. Reddish-brown clumps and filaments of dust partially obscure the galaxy's full face, while red, blue, and orange light from distant galaxies shines through its diffuse outer regions and dots the inky-black background.

science.title: New economics study finds that ICE activity has upended the US childcare workforce
science.summary: When U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations come to town, it can create a landscape of fear, chilling commerce and school attendance, and now, new research shows that it affects childcare workers.

science.title: Bizarre Venus surface formations puzzle planetary scientists
science.summary: Bizarre Venus surface formations (or coronae) are likely key to understanding our twin planet's heretofore inscrutable interior. Using NASA Magellan spacecraft data from decades past, Anna Gulcher, an Earth and planetary scientist at Germany's University of Freiburg, has created innovative new 3D models of the largest coronae to better understand Venus' puzzling geodynamics.

science.title: Southern Ocean intermediate waters may hold key to Earth's carbon dioxide history
science.summary: Researchers at National Taiwan University and partner institutions have uncovered new evidence that Antarctic Intermediate Water (AAIW)—a distinct layer sitting 500–1,500 meters below the ocean surface—played a pivotal role in a major atmospheric carbon dioxide transition that occurred roughly 450,000 years ago.

science.title: Small financial changes can have big impact on stress, study finds
science.summary: New research is shedding light on the factors surrounding financial stress, showing that even small changes in income or expenses can significantly affect how stressed people feel, both at home and at work.

science.title: Worker bumble bees help determine which baby bee will become queen
science.summary: Every bumble bee colony has a queen, but a new study led by researchers at Penn State suggests the process of determining which baby bee reigns supreme may be less monarchal than the royal title suggests. The study, published in the journal Insect Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, explored why some bumble bee larvae become workers and others become queens, despite coming from the same eggs.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Findings reconsider the existence of Europa's vapor plumes
mix_items.summary: Looking back at 14 years of Hubble telescope data for Jupiter's moon Europa has given Southwest Research Institute (SwRI) scientists a better understanding of its tenuous atmosphere. The findings have cast doubt on previous evidence suggesting that the icy moon intermittently discharges faint water plumes from a presumed subsurface ocean.

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Professional chess analysis reveals faster decisions correlate with higher quality moves
mix_items.summary: In chess, faster decisions are on average of higher quality. This is the conclusion of a study that has just been published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. The team of researchers, which, in addition to Professor Uwe Sunde from LMU, includes scientists from Erasmus University Rotterdam and UniDistance Suisse, analyzed data from professional games of chess. Their aim was to investigate how the time taken to make a complex strategic decision is related to the quality of this decision. Sunde and his colleagues believe the outcome of their research indicates that the decision time reflects the subjectively perceived difficulty of the problem, which can vary depending on the situation.

mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Is Earth's constant companion a stray asteroid or a chunk of the moon?
mix_items.summary: Earth has a group of cosmic stalkers. Known as "co-orbitals," these small bits of rock have a 1:1 mean motion resonance with Earth. Basically, they take the exact same amount of time to orbit the sun as we do. Astronomers have long believed these objects wandered in from the main asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, but recent spectral analysis suggests they better match the space-weathered lunar silicates that make up the moon's surface. As such, there has been an ongoing debate about whether these cosmic stalkers are actually visitors from the belt or blasted pieces of the moon. A new study, published in Icarus, from researchers Elisa Alessi and Robert Jedicke provides strong hints that the belt is the more likely source—but pretty soon we'll get a definitive answer from a spacecraft.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Hubble reveals rare galaxy 100 million light-years away caught in transition
mix_items.summary: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image reveals an enigmatic galaxy with a bright center and a face that hints at spiral structure, yet it holds no obvious spiral arms. Reddish-brown clumps and filaments of dust partially obscure the galaxy's full face, while red, blue, and orange light from distant galaxies shines through its diffuse outer regions and dots the inky-black background.

mix_items.title: New economics study finds that ICE activity has upended the US childcare workforce
mix_items.summary: When U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations come to town, it can create a landscape of fear, chilling commerce and school attendance, and now, new research shows that it affects childcare workers.

mix_items.title: Bizarre Venus surface formations puzzle planetary scientists
mix_items.summary: Bizarre Venus surface formations (or coronae) are likely key to understanding our twin planet's heretofore inscrutable interior. Using NASA Magellan spacecraft data from decades past, Anna Gulcher, an Earth and planetary scientist at Germany's University of Freiburg, has created innovative new 3D models of the largest coronae to better understand Venus' puzzling geodynamics.

mix_items.title: Southern Ocean intermediate waters may hold key to Earth's carbon dioxide history
mix_items.summary: Researchers at National Taiwan University and partner institutions have uncovered new evidence that Antarctic Intermediate Water (AAIW)—a distinct layer sitting 500–1,500 meters below the ocean surface—played a pivotal role in a major atmospheric carbon dioxide transition that occurred roughly 450,000 years ago.

mix_items.title: Small financial changes can have big impact on stress, study finds
mix_items.summary: New research is shedding light on the factors surrounding financial stress, showing that even small changes in income or expenses can significantly affect how stressed people feel, both at home and at work.

mix_items.title: Worker bumble bees help determine which baby bee will become queen
mix_items.summary: Every bumble bee colony has a queen, but a new study led by researchers at Penn State suggests the process of determining which baby bee reigns supreme may be less monarchal than the royal title suggests. The study, published in the journal Insect Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, explored why some bumble bee larvae become workers and others become queens, despite coming from the same eggs.

mix_items.title: ATS: City-Wide mold intervention can reduce ED asthma visits
mix_items.summary: A city-wide mold intervention in public housing can reduce asthma emergency department (ED) visits, according to a study presented at the American Thoracic Society 2026 International Conference, held from May 15 to 20 in Orlando, Florida.

mix_items.title: Prehospital trauma care gets flexible option as whole blood matches components in nationwide trial
mix_items.summary: Giving whole blood or the component parts of blood are equally effective options for paramedics and emergency medical technicians to use in treating patients with severe, traumatic bleeding before arriving at the hospital, according to a large, nationwide trial directed by University of Pittsburgh and UPMC clinicians and scientists.

mix_items.title: New-onset A-Fib may accelerate kidney function decline
mix_items.summary: New-onset atrial fibrillation (AF) may accelerate kidney function decline, according to a study published online May 14 in JAMA Network Open.

mix_items.title: More than a third of menopausal women lose sleep to hot flashes, waking during the night
mix_items.summary: While sleep is essential to health, women face unique challenges to getting quality sleep across key life stages. According to new survey results from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine, more than a third of women ages 45–64 report frequent nighttime waking (37%) and night sweats or hot flashes that disrupt sleep (35%) due to menopause, while more than a quarter have increased daytime fatigue (29%) and trouble falling asleep (27%). Only 6% of women in that age group say their sleep quality has not changed during menopause.

mix_items.title: From PCOS to PMOS: What the name change to polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome means for women's health
mix_items.summary: "Hopeful," "excited" and "helpful" for the future of women's health—these are just some of the words expressed by the team that worked together for more than a decade to change the name polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) to polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS).

mix_items.title: Why brain cells learn better: NMDA receptor maps may explain memory-linked calcium flow
mix_items.summary: The human brain constantly adapts in response to experiences, forming new connections between neurons and reorganizing existing ones. The brain's ability to adapt in response to experiences is known as neuroplasticity.

mix_items.title: Key multiple sclerosis models reveal different damage timelines, offering roadmap for myelin repair studies
mix_items.summary: More than 1 million people across the United States live with multiple sclerosis (MS), a disease that affects the brain, optic nerves, and spine. MS is an unpredictable disorder, with symptoms—such as overwhelming fatigue, muscle spasms, and vision problems—flaring up and then subsiding over days, months, or even years. To identify new treatment paradigms for MS, studying the underlying damage to the nervous system is key.

mix_items.title: Flavored vapes led to a major shake‑up at the FDA
mix_items.summary: The resignation of Marty Makary, commissioner of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, on May 12, 2026, brought to the forefront a heated controversy over fruit-flavored nicotine vapes.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Daca as primi bani pentru fiecare data cand ma razgandesc, as investi prudent.

satire_ro.title: Bestia cu chip uman! El e singurul român care nu i-a ținut pumnii Alexandrei Căpitănescu
